Anduril will do a couple billion in revenue this year.
Metaview customers close roles 30% faster using their AI recruiting platform.
Anthropic may now be at a $22 billion run rate in revenue, which is consistent with the Ramp spending shift data.
The TerraFab chip volume is expected to be 20% Tesla and 80% SpaceX/data centers.
Starlink reportedly has 53% profit margins and is wildly profitable at scale.
Jonathan (Groq founder) is expected to make about $950 million after double taxation from the Nvidia deal.
Chamath reportedly made $950 million from the Groq/Nvidia deal, though Chamath himself said on Twitter he made much more.
Token spend as a percentage of revenue for some AI apps is 5-8%, while for coding apps it may be 40-50%.
Jason Lemkin described running AI agents (VP of marketing, VP of customer success) on Claude Sonnet 4.7 and some Opus, costing somewhere in the range of $200 to $10,000 per month in tokens.
The effective tax rate to the founders is roughly 60% and they can't hold Nvidia stock.
Anthropic is doing $22 billion in the blink of an eye, with people lining up to buy agentic products.
Spark paid $4 billion pre-revenue for Anthropic, and it paid off.
In 2014 when Steve left Microsoft, his net worth was $20 billion, almost entirely comprised of Microsoft stock. Today, 11 years later, it is a staggering $130 billion according to Forbes.
In 2014 when Steve left Microsoft, his net worth was $20 billion, almost entirely comprised of Microsoft stock.
Microsoft ends 1992 at $2.8 billion in revenue.
By spring quarter of the 1997-98 academic year, Google was doing 10,000 queries a day.
Google (Alphabet) generates more net income or profit than any other US company, more than Apple, Microsoft, ExxonMobil, J.P. Morgan Chase, and Berkshire Hathaway.
Jeff Bezos's stake in Google, if he never sold, is estimated to be worth about $20 billion today.
If Jeff Bezos sold at Google's IPO, he would have turned his $250,000 seed investment into approximately $200 million.
Google's Series A raised $25 million at a $100 million post-money valuation, split between Sequoia (Michael Moritz) and Kleiner Perkins (John Doerr), each receiving 12.5%.
Yahoo paid Google $7.2 million for organic search results in 2001, the first full year of the Yahoo portal search deal.
Yahoo's revenue from Overture-powered paid search was approximately 75% of Yahoo's overall company revenue.
In 2001, Google did $86 million in revenue and $10 million in profit, turning profitable that year.
In 2002, Google did $440 million of revenue, up roughly 5x-6x from the $86 million they did in 2001.
Google's international revenue share grew from 18% in 2002 to 22% in 2002 accrued, and 29% in 2003.
AOL had 34 million users at the time of the Google-AOL deal in summer 2002.
AOL made $35 million in the first half year of the Google deal alone in 2002, and then $200 million in 2003.
Terry Semel, CEO of Yahoo, offered to buy Google for $1 billion in late 2001, knowing Yahoo was Google's biggest customer paying less than $10 million.
Search is today a half a trillion dollar annual revenue market.
AdSense launched at the end of Q1 2003, and by the end of the year, just a few months later, it's doing over $1 million dollars a day in revenue.
Google's 2002 revenue was $440 million with $185 million in profits.
Google's 2002 operating income was $185 million.
In 2003, Google's revenue exploded to $1.5 billion with almost $350 million in operating income.
Google's 2003 operating income was almost $350 million, up from $185 million the prior year.
AdSense added $0.5 billion in 2003 at much lower margin.
Microsoft's P/E ratio is 37 as of the time of recording.
NVIDIA's P/E ratio is 46 as of the time of recording.

Meta's P/E ratio is 27 as of the time of recording.
By 2007, Google was using 200 different pieces of information to determine the ranking on a query.
Google full year 2005 revenue was $6.1 billion, almost double the $3.1 billion in 2004.
Google's 2004 full year revenue was $3.1 billion, the first year it went public.
Google bought YouTube for $1.6 billion in 2006.
Following Google's Q4 2005 earnings announcement showing flat earnings, the stock fell 27% in January 2006.
Estimates are that Google Maps does well over $5 billion in revenue, maybe even $10 billion in revenue.
Digital advertising would not eclipse TV advertising until 2017–2018, roughly 15 years after Google started working on video.
Google acquired YouTube in November 2006 for $1.65 billion in stock, less than 18 months after the product launched.
YouTube ad revenue tripled in 2009 in one year, according to Google's disclosure.
YouTube was estimated to make about $4 billion in revenue in 2012 and was roughly break-even.
In 2007, YouTube consumed as much bandwidth as the entire internet did in the year 2000.
In 2014, YouTube was 20% of the bits on the Internet, according to Shishir Mehrotra.
Google's total investment in YouTube was approximately $6.7 billion ($1.7B purchase price plus ~$5B in additional operating losses), and the asset now generates $8 billion in annual profit.
YouTube revenue is growing 10% to 15% every year.
Google's Docs and Sheets have roughly 500 million to 1 billion users each, while Microsoft Office has a couple hundred million users worldwide.
Microsoft Office has a couple hundred million users worldwide.
Google bought YouTube in October 2006 for $1.65 billion.
Google bought DoubleClick in April 2007 for $3.1 billion in cash.
About $30 billion of Google's 2024 revenue is from Google Network (display ads, including DoubleClick-related business).
Google had $3 billion in revenue in 2004, doubling to $6 billion in 2005.
In 2007, when Google launched Universal Search, they did $16.5 billion in revenue, becoming the largest seller of advertisements in the world.
Microsoft made a bid to buy Yahoo for $44 billion in February 2008.
Google's incremental gross margin on search revenue is like 98% or something like that.
Google bought Android for $50 million in July 2005.
Apple and the iPhone went from not in the smartphone market at all to over 50% market share of smartphones basically overnight.
Google's TPUs are the only real scale deployment of AI chips in the world besides NVIDIA GPUs, with AMD possibly third.
PHIL, Google's early language model, was using 15% of Google's entire data center infrastructure by the mid-2000s.
Google is estimated to have 2–3 million TPUs today.
NVIDIA shipped somewhere around 4 million GPUs last year.
Incorporating LSTMs into Google Translate in 2016 reduced the error rate by 60%.
Almost all the big AI companies use WorkOS today, including OpenAI, Anthropic, Cursor, Perplexity, Sierra, Replit, and Vercel, along with hundreds of other AI startups.
Noam Shazeer's Character.AI ended up with Google via a licensing, IP, and hiring agreement on the order of a few billion dollars.
After that, Microsoft invests another $2 billion in OpenAI, which seemed like a lot of money at the time. That takes us to the end of 2021.
January 2023 when OpenAI hits 100 million registered users for ChatGPT, Microsoft announces they're investing another $10 billion in OpenAI, and says that they now own 49% of the for-profit entity.
ChatGPT gets one million users within less than a week of launch on November 30, 2022.
By the end of December 31, 2022, ChatGPT has 30 million users.
By the end of January 2023, two months after launch, ChatGPT crosses 100 million registered users, the fastest product in history to hit that milestone.
Google's high watermark was November 19th of 2021 at right at $2 trillion of market cap. About a year after that slide began, they were worth a trillion dollars, nearly a 50% drawdown.

In April of 2024, Google was processing 10 trillion tokens across all their surfaces. In April of 2025, that was almost 500 trillion. That's a 50x increase in one year of the number of tokens that they're vending out across Google services through inference.
In April of 2024, Google was processing 10 trillion tokens across all their surfaces.
Between April of 2025 and June 2025, Google's token processing went from a little under 500 trillion to a little under one quadrillion tokens (technically 980 trillion).

Broadcom has something like a 50% margin when working with Google on the TPU versus NVIDIA's 80%. A 50% gross margin from your supplier or an 80% gross margin from your supplier is the difference between a 2x markup and a 5x markup.
I've seen estimates that over half the cost of running an AI data center is the chips and the associated depreciation.
Human cost (R&D, AI researchers, software engineering) is approximately 25%–33% of the cost of running an AI data center.
Power is actually a very small part of AI data center costs — approximately 2%–6%.
Google makes something like $400-ish per user per year just based on some napkin math in the US.
Google owns 90% of the search market. In AI, Google's market share is not 90% — possibly around 25%, maybe up to 50%.

When Dan Bane joined Trader Joe's in the late 1990s, they had just hit $1 billion in revenue.
Over the last 20 years or so, Trader Joe's has grown revenue at a little over 11% per year, putting estimated revenue at $24–25 billion today.
Crusoe's Managed Inference delivers up to 10 times faster time to first token compared to alternatives.
The NFL was already making like 50 million plus a year in broadcast rights by the mid-1970s.
